roof_system: Likely smooth single-ply membrane roof, possibly EPDM or TPO/PVC, with one lighter adjacent low-slope roof field that appears to be a separate membrane-coated/white roof surface

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Main roof field is dark, smooth, and low-slope with broad uniform panel-like appearance typical of a black membrane roof seen from aerial imagery.
Aerial view is too limited to distinguish a dark membrane from other single-ply systems, but the main field could be a single-ply roof with seams too small to resolve.
The adjacent white low-slope roof field appears reflective and uniform, consistent with a coated membrane or white single-ply surface from above.

roof_age_estimate: Likely original or near-original to a building from 1974, though the visible roof surface may have been replaced or recoated later

notes: Main roof field shows localized lighter/darker patches and small discoloration areas that may reflect repairs, staining, or drainage-related wear.
Several rooftop penetrations/equipment curbs are visible, increasing flashing and seal maintenance risk.
No obvious tree overhang or heavy debris is visible in the roof area.
No clear large-scale ponding is visible in this aerial image, but flat roofs can hide subtle drainage issues at this resolution.

observations: Main roof is a dark low-slope field with a fairly uniform membrane-like appearance.
Several small rooftop penetrations and equipment pads/curbs are visible across the roof.
Localized lighter patches and minor discoloration are visible on the main roof surface.
An adjacent lower roof field to the south appears much lighter/white and may be a different roof surface or a coated membrane.
Roof perimeter and parapet/edge details are visible but fine flashing condition cannot be confirmed from aerial imagery alone.
summary: Aerial imagery dated 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018 shows a low-slope commercial roof with a dark main membrane field and an adjacent lighter low-slope roof area. The main roof appears generally serviceable but has localized discoloration/patchy areas and multiple roof penetrations, which increase leak and flashing-maintenance risk. No clear large ponding is visible, though drainage performance cannot be confirmed from this view. Overall condition appears fair with moderate low
recommendation: Have a qualified commercial roofing contractor perform an onsite inspection to confirm the roof system, evaluate flashing and penetration seals, and test for moisture if leaks or soft areas are suspected. If the roof is an apparently restorable low-slope membrane and not asphalt shingle, evaluate silicone roof restoration before a more costly overlay or full replacement; a qualifying silicone system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, improve resistance to UV exposure and hail, withstand ponding water when properly designed and installed, and may qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If field testing shows widespread moisture, insulation failure, deck deterioration, or incompatible substrate conditions, an overlay or replacement evaluation is more appropriate. Confirm system eligibility, needed repairs, preparation scope, and project pricing.
